name: CS Fundamentals
description: Data structures, algorithms, OS concepts, systems design вҖ” LeetCode meets CS theory

data-structures.name: Data Structures
data-structures.description: Arrays, linked lists, trees, hash tables, heaps

topics.title: Arrays vs Linked Lists
topics.explainer:
## Arrays vs Linked Lists

### Arrays
- **Contiguous memory** вҖ” elements stored side by side
- **O(1) random access** by index
- **O(n) insertion/deletion** (must shift elements)
- Cache-friendly due to spatial locality

### Linked Lists
- **Non-contiguous** вҖ” each node points to the next
- **O(n) random access** (must traverse)
- **O(1) insertion/deletion** at known position
- Dynamic size, no resizing needed

### Dynamic Arrays (ArrayList/vector)
- Start with capacity k, double when full
- Amortized O(1) append
- Wasted space вүӨ 50%
topics.visual:
Array (contiguous):
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ 5 в”Ӯ 2 в”Ӯ 8 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ 9 в”Ӯ  O(1) index
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  Linked List (pointers):
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”җ   в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ 5 в”ӮвҶ’в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’в”Ӯ 2 в”ӮвҶ’в”ңв”Җв”ҖвҶ’в”Ӯ 8 в”ӮвҲ…в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҳ   в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”ҳ
  O(n) index, O(1) insert at pos

questions.question: Time complexity of accessing k-th element in a singly linked list?
questions.explanation: Must traverse from head through k nodes вҶ’ O(k).

questions.question: Why are arrays more cache-friendly than linked lists?
questions.explanation: Contiguous storage means accessing one element loads nearby ones into cache (spatial locality).

questions.question: Amortized time of appending to a dynamic array?
questions.explanation: Doubling strategy: most appends O(1), occasional O(n) resize вҶ’ amortized O(1).

coding_challenge.prompt: Implement a function that reverses a singly linked list in-place.
coding_challenge.solution:
def reverse_list(head):
    prev = None
    curr = head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev = curr
        curr = nxt
    return prev
coding_challenge.tests:
head = ListNode(1, ListNode(2, ListNode(3)))
result = reverse_list(head)
assert result.val == 3 and result.next.val == 2

topics.title: Hash Tables & Collision Resolution
topics.explainer:
## Hash Tables

### Core Idea
Map keys to indices: index = hash(key) % capacity
Average O(1) for get, put, delete.

### Collision Resolution
**Chaining:** Each bucket stores a linked list. On collision, append.
**Open Addressing:** Probe for next slot (linear, quadratic, double hashing).

### Load Factor
Оұ = n/capacity. When Оұ > 0.75, resize (double + rehash all).

### Worst Case
All keys hash to same bucket вҶ’ O(n). Good hash functions minimize this.
topics.visual:
Hash Table with Chaining:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  0  в”Ӯ вҶ’ (k1,v1) вҶ’ вҲ…    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  1  в”Ӯ вҶ’ вҲ…              в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  2  в”Ӯ вҶ’ (k2,v2) вҶ’ (k5,v5)
  в”Ӯ  3  в”Ӯ вҶ’ (k3,v3) вҶ’ вҲ…    в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: Worst-case time for hash table lookup?
questions.explanation: If all keys hash to same bucket вҶ’ O(n) traversal.

questions.question: What triggers a hash table resize?
questions.explanation: Resize when load factor (n/capacity) exceeds threshold (typically 0.75).

topics.title: Binary Trees & BSTs
topics.explainer:
## Binary Search Trees

### BST Property
For every node: left subtree < node < right subtree

### Traversals
- **Inorder** (L,Root,R) вҶ’ sorted order for BST
- **Preorder** (Root,L,R) вҶ’ copy/serialize
- **Postorder** (L,R,Root) вҶ’ deletion
- **Level-order** (BFS)

### Balanced BST: O(log n) operations
- AVL: height-balanced (|О”h| вүӨ 1)
- Red-Black: color-balanced (Java TreeMap, C++ map)

### Worst Case
Skewed/degenerate BST вҶ’ O(n) вҖ” essentially a linked list.

questions.question: Which BST traversal gives sorted order?
questions.explanation: Inorder (L,Root,R) visits nodes in ascending order for a BST.

questions.question: Height of a balanced BST with n nodes?
questions.explanation: Balanced BST halves search space each level вҶ’ height O(log n).

questions.question: Deleting a BST node with two children: replace with?
questions.explanation: Replace with inorder successor (smallest in right subtree) to maintain BST property.

topics.title: Heaps & Priority Queues
topics.explainer:
## Heaps

### Min-Heap Property
Parent вүӨ both children (root = minimum)

### Max-Heap Property
Parent вүҘ both children (root = maximum)

### Operations
- **Insert:** Add at bottom, bubble up вҖ” O(log n)
- **Extract min/max:** Remove root, move last to root, bubble down вҖ” O(log n)
- **Peek:** O(1)
- **Build heap:** O(n) using heapify

### Array Representation
For index i: left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2, parent = (i-1)/2

### Applications
- Priority queues, Dijkstra's algorithm
- Heap sort: O(n log n), in-place
- Finding k-th largest element: O(n log k)

questions.question: Time complexity to build a heap from n elements?
questions.explanation: Bottom-up heapify is O(n) вҖ” most nodes are near bottom and need little work.

questions.question: In a max-heap, the largest element is at:
questions.explanation: Max-heap property: parent вүҘ children, so root is maximum.

algorithms.name: Algorithms
algorithms.description: Sorting, searching, dynamic programming, graph algorithms

topics.title: Sorting Algorithms Compared
topics.explainer:
## Sorting Algorithms

### Comparison Sorts
- **Merge Sort:** O(n log n) always, stable, O(n) space
- **Quick Sort:** O(n log n) avg, O(nВІ) worst, in-place, fastest in practice
- **Heap Sort:** O(n log n) always, in-place, not stable
- **Insertion Sort:** O(nВІ), stable, great for small/nearly-sorted arrays

### Key Insights
- Lower bound for comparison sorts: О©(n log n)
- Quick sort wins in practice (cache-friendly, small constants)
- Merge sort for guaranteed performance
- Non-comparison: Counting O(n+k), Radix O(d(n+k))

### Stability
A stable sort preserves relative order of equal elements.

questions.question: Which sort has best worst-case time complexity?
options: Merge sort вҖ” O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Merge sort guarantees O(n log n) in all cases.

questions.question: Which is generally fastest in practice for large random arrays?
questions.explanation: Quick sort has excellent cache locality and small constant factors.

questions.question: A stable sort preserves:
options: O(n log n) in all cases
questions.explanation: Equal elements maintain their original relative ordering.

topics.title: Dynamic Programming
topics.explainer:
## Dynamic Programming

### Core Idea
Break problem into overlapping subproblems, solve each once, store results.

### Approaches
- **Top-down (Memoization):** Recursive + cache
- **Bottom-up (Tabulation):** Iterative, fill table from base cases

### Classic Problems
- **Fibonacci:** O(n) with DP vs O(2вҒҝ) naive
- **0/1 Knapsack:** dp[i][w] = max value, O(nW)
- **LCS:** dp[i][j] = longest common subsequence, O(mn)
- **Climbing Stairs:** dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2]

### Recognition
Look for: 'count ways', 'min/max', 'is it possible' with optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems.
topics.visual:
Fibonacci DP:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ 0 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ 1 в”Ӯ 2 в”Ӯ 3 в”Ӯ 5 в”Ӯ 8 в”Ӯ13 в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҙв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
  Each = sum of previous two

  0/1 Knapsack:
       w=0  1  2  3  4  5
  i=0 [ 0  0  0  0  0  0 ]
  i=1 [ 0  1  1  1  1  1 ]
  i=2 [ 0  1  6  7  7  7 ]

questions.question: Two properties that indicate a DP solution?
questions.explanation: DP needs both: optimal solution from optimal sub-solutions + same subproblems solved repeatedly.

questions.question: 0/1 Knapsack with n items and capacity W: time complexity?
questions.explanation: DP table: n rows Г— W columns, each O(1) вҶ’ O(nW) pseudo-polynomial.

coding_challenge.prompt: Climbing stairs: you can take 1 or 2 steps. How many ways to reach step n?
coding_challenge.tests:
assert climb_stairs(1) == 1
assert climb_stairs(3) == 3
assert climb_stairs(5) == 8

topics.title: Graph Algorithms: BFS & DFS
topics.explainer:
## Graph Algorithms

### BFS (Breadth-First Search)
- Uses a **queue**, explores level by level
- Finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
- Time: O(V + E)

### DFS (Depth-First Search)
- Uses a **stack** (or recursion)
- Goes as deep as possible first
- Used for: topological sort, cycle detection, connected components

### Dijkstra's Algorithm
- Shortest path in weighted graphs (non-negative edges)
- Uses min-heap / priority queue
- Time: O((V+E) log V)

### Topological Sort
- Linear ordering: for edge uвҶ’v, u before v
- Only on DAGs
- DFS post-order then reverse
topics.visual:
BFS (level-by-level):     DFS (deep first):
       A                      A
      / \                    / \
     B   C                  B   C
    / \   \                /
   D   E   F              D вҶ’ E вҶ’ F

  BFS: A,B,C,D,E,F       DFS: A,B,D,E,C,F

questions.question: Which finds shortest path in unweighted graphs?
questions.explanation: BFS explores level by level вҶ’ first visit = shortest path (by edges).

questions.question: What data structure does BFS use?
questions.explanation: BFS uses a queue (FIFO) for level-by-level processing.

questions.question: Time complexity of BFS/DFS on a graph with V vertices and E edges?
questions.explanation: Visit each vertex once O(V) and each edge once O(E) вҶ’ O(V+E).

topics.title: Processes vs Threads
topics.explainer:
## Processes vs Threads

### Process
- Independent execution unit with own memory space
- Own: address space, file descriptors, stack, heap
- Context switch: expensive (save/restore address space)

### Thread
- Lightweight unit within a process
- **Shares:** address space, heap, file descriptors
- **Own:** stack, registers, program counter
- Context switch: cheaper

### Concurrency Issues
- **Race condition:** Result depends on timing of thread access
- **Deadlock:** Threads waiting for each other's locks
- **Starvation:** Thread never gets CPU time

### Synchronization
- **Mutex:** Only 1 thread in critical section
- **Semaphore:** Up to n threads
- **Condition variable:** Wait for condition
topics.visual:
Process vs Thread:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

  в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ Shared: Code,Data,Filesв”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ӮStk1в”Ӯ в”ӮStk2в”Ӯ в”ӮStk3в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в”ӮReg1в”Ӯ в”ӮReg2в”Ӯ в”ӮReg3в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ  в”Ӯ  в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ   в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: What do threads within a process share?
questions.explanation: Threads share address space, heap, files. Each has own stack and registers.

questions.question: Deadlock requires all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Deadlock needs: mutual exclusion, hold&wait, NO preemption, circular wait.

questions.question: Mutex vs Semaphore:
options: Mutex: 1 thread; Semaphore: up to n
questions.explanation: Mutex is binary (1 thread). Semaphore has counter allowing up to n.

topics.title: Virtual Memory & Paging
topics.explainer:
## Virtual Memory

### Concept
Each process sees contiguous virtual address space, mapped to physical memory by OS+MMU.

### Paging
- Virtual memory divided into **pages** (typically 4KB)
- Physical memory divided into **frames**
- Page table maps pages вҶ’ frames

### Page Faults
Access to page not in RAM:
1. CPU traps to OS
2. OS finds page on disk
3. Loads into free frame
4. Updates page table
5. Restarts instruction

### Page Replacement
- **FIFO:** Replace oldest
- **LRU:** Replace least recently used (best practical)
- **Optimal:** Replace page needed furthest in future (theoretical)

### TLB
Cache for page table entries вҶ’ O(1) translation on hit.
topics.visual:
Virtual вҶ’ Physical:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ    в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ Page 0  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ Frame 3    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Page 1  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ Frame 7    в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ Page 2  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ [on disk]  в”Ӯ вҶҗ fault!
  в”Ӯ Page 3  в”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”ҖвҶ’ в”Ӯ Frame 1    в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ    в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: What happens on a page fault?
options: OS loads page from disk into a frame
questions.explanation: OS loads missing page from disk, updates page table, restarts instruction.

questions.question: Theoretically optimal page replacement?
options: Replace page not needed for longest time
questions.explanation: Belady's optimal: replace page needed furthest in future (requires future knowledge).

topics.title: CPU Scheduling Algorithms
topics.explainer:
## CPU Scheduling

### Algorithms
- **FCFS:** Simple queue. Problem: convoy effect.
- **SJF:** Optimal avg waiting time. Problem: starvation of long jobs.
- **Round Robin:** Time quantum per process. Fair for interactive.
- **Priority:** Each process has priority. Fix starvation with aging.
- **Multilevel Feedback Queue:** Modern OS approach.

### Metrics
- **Turnaround time:** completion вҲ’ arrival
- **Waiting time:** turnaround вҲ’ burst
- **Response time:** first run вҲ’ arrival

### Preemptive vs Non-preemptive
- Preemptive: OS can interrupt running process
- Non-preemptive: process runs until done or blocked
topics.visual:
Round Robin (quantum=4):
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ  P1: burst=10           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  P2: burst=4            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  P3: burst=6            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  |P1|P2|P3|P1|P3|P1|   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  0  4  8  12 16 18 20  в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: Which minimizes average waiting time?
questions.explanation: SJF is provably optimal for minimizing average waiting time.

questions.question: How does Round Robin prevent starvation?
options: Every process gets a guaranteed time quantum
questions.explanation: RR gives every process equal time quantum in rotation.

systems-design.name: Systems Design
systems-design.description: Scalability, CAP theorem, caching, load balancing

topics.title: CAP Theorem
topics.explainer:
## CAP Theorem

### Three Guarantees (pick 2)
- **Consistency:** Every read gets most recent write
- **Availability:** Every request gets a response
- **Partition Tolerance:** Works despite network splits

### During a Partition
- **CP:** Reject requests for consistency (HBase, MongoDB)
- **AP:** Serve stale data for availability (Cassandra, DynamoDB)
- **CA:** Only without partitions (single-node)

### Real World
- Banking: CP (consistency critical)
- Social media: AP (stale feed OK)
- DNS: AP

### PACELC
No partition: trade Latency vs Consistency.
topics.visual:
CAP Triangle:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”Ӯ       C              в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ      / \             в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ     / CP\            в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ    /     \           в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ   /  CA   \          в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ  Pв”Җв”Җв”ҖAPв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҖA         в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ                      в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ CP: HBase, MongoDB   в”Ӯ
  в”Ӯ AP: Cassandra, Dynamoв”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: During a network partition, a CP system will:
questions.explanation: CP sacrifices availability to maintain consistency during partitions.

questions.question: Why can't a distributed system have all three CAP properties?
options: During partition, must choose between C and A
questions.explanation: During partition, nodes can't communicate вҖ” must choose wait (lose A) or serve stale (lose C).

topics.title: Caching & Load Balancing
topics.explainer:
## Caching & Load Balancing

### Cache Layers
1. Browser cache (HTTP headers)
2. CDN (geographic)
3. Application cache (Redis, Memcached)
4. Database cache (query cache)

### Cache Strategies
- **Write-through:** Write cache + DB simultaneously
- **Write-back:** Write cache, async to DB
- **Write-around:** Write DB only, cache on read

### Eviction
- **LRU:** Least Recently Used (most common)
- **LFU:** Least Frequently Used
- **TTL:** Time To Live

### Load Balancing
- Round Robin, Least Connections, IP Hash, Weighted
topics.visual:
Architecture:
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ

  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

  в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”ҳ
  в”Ңв”Җв”Җв–јв”җв”Ңв–јв”Җв”җв”Ңв–јв”Җв”Җв”җ
  в”ӮS1 в”Ӯв”ӮS2 в”Ӯв”ӮS3 в”Ӯ
  в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳв””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳв””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”җ
     в”ӮRedis в”Ӯ
     в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”ҳ
     в”Ңв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”җ
     в”Ӯ DB  в”Ӯ
     в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ

questions.question: Which LB strategy ensures client always reaches same server?
questions.explanation: IP Hash uses client IP hash for consistent routing (session affinity).

questions.question: Write-through caching writes to:
questions.explanation: Write-through ensures consistency by writing both simultaneously.

topics.title: Big-O Complexity Analysis
topics.explainer:
## Big-O Analysis

### Common (fast вҶ’ slow)
1. O(1) вҖ” constant
2. O(log n) вҖ” binary search
3. O(n) вҖ” linear scan
4. O(n log n) вҖ” merge sort
5. O(nВІ) вҖ” nested loops
6. O(2вҒҝ) вҖ” subsets
7. O(n!) вҖ” permutations

### Rules
- Drop constants: O(2n) вҶ’ O(n)
- Drop lower terms: O(nВІ+n) вҶ’ O(nВІ)
- Different inputs use different variables
- Nested loops multiply

### Space Complexity
- In-place: O(1) extra
- Recursion: O(depth) for call stack

### Amortized
Average cost over n operations (dynamic array append).

questions.question: Time complexity of binary search?
questions.explanation: Halves search space each step вҶ’ logвӮӮ(n) steps.

questions.explanation: n Г— n = O(nВІ).

topics.title: Two Pointers & Sliding Window
topics.explainer:
## Two Pointers & Sliding Window

### Two Pointers
Use two pointers moving toward each other or same direction.
- **Two Sum (sorted):** L+R pointers, adjust by sum
- **Container With Most Water:** Move shorter pointer
- **Remove Duplicates:** Slow/fast pointer

### Sliding Window
Maintain window [left, right], expand/contract.

**Template:**
```
left = 0
for right in range(len(arr)):
    add arr[right] to window
    while window invalid:
        remove arr[left], left += 1
    update answer
```

- Fixed-size: max sum of k consecutive
- Variable: longest substring without repeats
topics.visual:
Two Pointers (sorted Two Sum):
  [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11]  target=12
   LвҶ’              вҶҗR
   1+11=12 вң“ Found!

  Sliding Window (k=3):
  [2,1,5,1,3,2]
  [2,1,5]=8
    [1,5,1]=7
      [5,1,3]=9 вҶҗ max!
        [1,3,2]=6

questions.question: Best pattern for 'Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters'?
questions.explanation: Sliding window with a set: expand right, contract left on duplicate. O(n).

questions.question: In sorted two-pointer, if sum < target, you:
questions.explanation: Sum too small вҶ’ need bigger element вҶ’ advance left pointer right.

coding_challenge.prompt: Find maximum sum of any contiguous subarray of size k.
coding_challenge.solution:
def max_subarray_sum(arr, k):
    if len(arr) < k: return 0
    s = sum(arr[:k])
    mx = s
    for i in range(k, len(arr)):
        s += arr[i] - arr[i-k]
        mx = max(mx, s)
    return mx

topics.title: Backtracking & Recursion
topics.explainer:
## Backtracking

### Core Idea
Build solutions incrementally; abandon when invalid.

### Template
```
def backtrack(path, choices):
    if is_solution(path):
        results.append(path[:])
        return
    for choice in choices:
        if valid(choice, path):
            path.append(choice)    # choose
            backtrack(path, next)  # explore
            path.pop()             # unchoose
```

### Classic Problems
- N-Queens, Subsets (2вҒҝ), Permutations (n!)
- Sudoku Solver, Word Search

### Pruning
Skip branches early вҶ’ dramatically reduce search space.

questions.question: How many subsets does a set of n elements have?
questions.explanation: Each element: include or exclude вҶ’ 2 choices вҶ’ 2вҒҝ subsets.

questions.question: What is pruning in backtracking?
options: Skipping branches that can't lead to valid solutions
questions.explanation: Pruning cuts off impossible branches early, reducing the search space.
